perturbation.original_text: “Term ” means a period of 12 months commencing on the date of this Confidentiality Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: “Term ” means a period of 36 months commencing on the date of this Confidentiality Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Extended the term of the agreement to 36 months. While not inherently illegal, excessively long confidentiality terms can be challenged as unreasonable restraints on trade, particularly if they hinder an individual's ability to work in their field. Reasonableness depends on the circumstances, but some jurisdictions might view 3 years as potentially problematic depending on the sensitivity of the information.
perturbation.law_citation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally invalidates contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While confidentiality agreements are often an exception, a three-year term might be viewed as an unreasonable restraint if it unduly restricts the Recipient's ability to operate or compete, especially if the Evaluation Material becomes less relevant over time.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the confidentiality term to 36 months, which the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation suggest could be an unreasonable restraint of trade under California Business and Professions Code Section 16600. Scraped Law Snippet 1, BPC 16600(a), states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void," except as provided in the chapter. A three-year confidentiality term, while not explicitly illegal, might be viewed as an unreasonable restraint, especially if the Evaluation Material becomes less relevant over time or unduly restricts the Recipient's ability to compete. The modified contract creates a condition that could be deemed a violation of BPC 16600.

perturbation.original_text: Promptly upon the Disclosing Party ’s request , the Recipient will and will cause its Representatives to: (i) return to the Disclosing Party all Evaluation Material furnished to the Recipient or its Representatives, without retaining copie s or other reproductions, reports, extracts, notes or other memoranda thereof (whether electronic, magnetic or otherwise); (ii) destroy or have destroyed all reproductions, memoranda, notes, reports, extracts, compilations, analyses and documents and all d ocuments prepared by or in the possession of the Recipient or its Representatives related to the information contained in the Evaluation Material but which does not itself constitute Evaluation Material; and (iii) provide to the Disclosing Party an officer ’s certificate that the terms and conditions of this paragraph have been complied with.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the Disclosing Party ’s request, which must be made within 30 days of the termination of discussions regarding a possible transaction, the Recipient will and will cause its Representatives to: (i) return to the Disclosing Party all Evaluation Material furnished to the Recipient or its Representatives, without retaining copie s or other reproductions, reports, extracts, notes or other memoranda thereof (whether electronic, magnetic or otherwise); (ii) destroy or have destroyed all reproductions, memoranda, notes, reports, extracts, compilations, analyses and documents and all d ocuments prepared by or in the possession of the Recipient or its Representatives related to the information contained in the Evaluation Material but which does not itself constitute Evaluation Material; and (iii) provide to the Disclosing Party an officer ’s certificate that the terms and conditions of this paragraph have been complied with.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the timeframe for the Disclosing Party to request the return or destruction of confidential information to 30 days after termination of discussions. This could conflict with trade secret laws that require ongoing protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) requires the owner of a trade secret to take reasonable measures to protect its secrecy. Imposing a short 30-day window to request the return of confidential information might be seen as failing to take reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, especially if the information remains valuable beyond that period, therefore losing the right for trade secret protections.
perturbation.location: 4. Return or Destruction of Evaluation Materials:

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 30-day time limit for requesting the return of confidential information, which potentially contradicts the ongoing obligation to protect trade secrets as required by the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA). The Law Explanation posits that this limited window may be viewed as a failure to take reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 18 USC 1836, discusses civil proceedings related to trade secret misappropriation, suggesting a legal framework for protecting trade secrets. By limiting the timeframe, the changed text may weaken the protection afforded to trade secrets, creating a potential contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient will not, and the Recipient will cause its Representatives not to , solicit for hire or employment, directly or indirectly, any officer or employee of the Disclosing Party or its direct and indirect subsidiaries that the Recipient becomes aware of or is in contact with in Red River Oil Inc. Confidentiality Agreement Page 4 of 8 connection with its evaluation of a Transaction. For the purposes of this clause, “solicitation ͟ shall not include solicitation of any officer or employee of the Disclosing Party or its direct and indirect subsidiaries who is solicited: (i) by advertising in a newspaper or periodical of general circulat ion; or (ii) indirectly through a personnel search agency engaged by the Recipient generally (not specifically in respect of the Disclosing Party) provided that the Recipient shall not pursue hiring of any officer or employee of the Disclosing Party or its direct and indirect subsidiaries once the Recipient is advised that such person is an officer or employee of the Disclosing Party ; provided that this paragraph 8 shall cease to bind the Recipient on the expiry of the Term.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient will not, and the Recipient will cause its Representatives not to , solicit for hire or employment, directly or indirectly, any officer or employee of the Disclosing Party or its direct and indirect subsidiaries that the Recipient becomes aware of or is in contact with in connection with its evaluation of a Transaction. This paragraph 8 shall not cease to bind the Recipient, and will be indefinite.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the expiration date of the non-solicitation clause, making it indefinite. This could be seen as an unreasonable restraint on trade, depending on the jurisdiction and the specific circumstances.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_citation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. An indefinite non-solicitation clause is likely unenforceable in California, as it unreasonably restricts the employee's future employment opportunities and the Recipient's ability to hire.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the expiration date of the non-solicitation clause, making it indefinite. This contradicts California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." By making the non-solicitation agreement indefinite, the changed text imposes a restraint on trade that is likely unenforceable under California law, directly violating BPC 16600.

perturbation.original_text: 18. Jurisdiction: This Agreement will be gover ned by and construed in accordance with the laws of Alberta and b oth parties hereby irrevocably and unconditionally consent to and submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of Alberta for any actions, suits or proceedings arising out of the interp retation or enforcement of this Agreement (and both parties agree not to commence any action, suit or proceeding relating thereto except in such courts) and further agree that service of any process, summons, notice or document by personal delivery to its address set forth below shall be effective service of process for any action, suit or proceeding brought against the Recipient in any such court. Both parties hereby irrevocably and unconditionally waive any objection to the laying of venue of any action, suit or proceeding arising out of this Agreement or the matters contemplated hereby in the courts of Alberta and hereby further irrevocably and unconditionally waive and agree not to plead or claim in any such court that any such action, suit or proceedin g so brought has been brought in an inconvenient forum.
perturbation.changed_text: 18. Jurisdiction: This Agreement will be gover ned by and construed in accordance with the laws of Alberta and b oth parties hereby irrevocably and unconditionally consent to and submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of Alberta for any actions, suits or proceedings arising out of the interp retation or enforcement of this Agreement (and both parties agree not to commence any action, suit or proceeding relating thereto except in such courts).
perturbation.explanation: Removed the clause about service of process by personal delivery and waiving objections to venue. This might conflict with rules of civil procedure if the Recipient is located outside of Alberta, making it difficult to properly serve them and potentially leading to jurisdictional challenges.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 4
perturbation.law_citation: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 4
perturbation.law_explanation: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 4 governs service of process in federal courts. By removing the specific agreement on service of process and venue, the contract creates ambiguity regarding how to properly serve a Recipient located outside of Alberta. This can lead to challenges to the court's jurisdiction if service is not properly effected under Rule 4 or applicable state laws.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 4. Summons | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 4. Summons (a) Contents; Amendments. (1) Contents. A summons must: (A) name the court and the parties; (B) be directed to the defendant; (C) state the name and address of the plaintiff's attorney or—if unrepresented—of the plaintiff; (D) state the time within which the defendant must appear and defend; (E) notify the defendant that a failure to appear and defend will result in a default judgment against the defendant for the relief demanded in the complaint; (F) be signed by the clerk; and (G) bear the court's seal. (2) Amendments. The court may permit a summons to be amended. (b) Issuance. On or after filing the complaint, the plaintiff may present a summons to the clerk for signature and seal. If the summons is properly completed, the clerk must sign, seal, and issue it to the plaintiff for service on the defendant. A summons—or a copy of a summons that is addressed to multiple defendants—must be issued for each defendant to be served. (c) Service. (1) In General. A summons must be served with a copy of the complaint. The plaintiff is responsible for having the summons and complaint served within the time allowed by Rule 4(m) and must furnish the necessary copies to the person who makes service. (2) By Whom. Any person who is at least 18 years old and not a party may serve a summons and complaint. (3) By a Marshal or Someone Specially Appointed. At the plaintiff's request, the court may order that service be made by a United States marshal or deputy marshal or by a person specially appointed by the court. The court must so order if the plaintiff is authorized to proceed in forma pauperis under 28 U.S.C. §1915 or as a seaman under 28 U.S.C. §1916 . (d) Waiving Service. (1) Requesting a Waiver. An individual, corporation, or association that is subject to service under Rule 4(e) , (f) , or (h) has a duty to avoid unnecessary
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the clause about service of process by personal delivery and waiving objections to venue, which creates a potential contradiction with the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure. The Law Explanation states that this omission "creates ambiguity regarding how to properly serve a Recipient located outside of Alberta," potentially leading to jurisdictional challenges if service is not properly effected under Rule 4. Scraped Law Snippet 1, discussing Rule 4, covers the contents of a summons, who can serve it, and waiving service. By removing the clause defining effective service, the contract opens itself up to potential disputes over whether service was properly executed according to Rule 4, creating a legal contradiction under Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction, because the modified contract could be seen as not meeting the requirements of properly serving a summons.
